--- Thread (1 messages, 2018-06-20) ---
[2018-06-20 06:03] .jrkirby (jrkirby): Prismata Incremental:

You play games against pacifist bot. You go first, you both have 6 drones and 2 engineers. Base set. Seems boring, right? But the game does not end when you destroy master bot's units. No, the game ends turn 20, no matter what. The goal? To get as much gold as possible in the bank before then.

But that's not the end. That, is the beginning. You enter a shop. All kinds of things to buy: new blueprints, extra units to start with, stuff like that. You can also increase the difficulty: both increasing the AI aggressiveness and skill, and also giving it a more powerful start position. Why? Doing so increases your multiplier exponentially increasing how much gold you get to take from the end of your net match into the shop. And of course the price of items in the shop has an exponential nature to it's pricing as well; to afford the expensive items you must either beat the bot on very difficult settings, and have a ton of gold left over, or you have to beat the bot over and over and over again.

This continues ad infinitum, as incremental games do, with progress getting slower and slower each time you complete the loop. When you are finally tired of it, you wonder why you spent 45 hours on something so pointless, there having been no real finale, no true goal, just an ever increasingly time consuming list of milestones you reached. There's another milestone you could reach, but it's been mathematically placed 3-4 more hours in the future and you've worn yourself out.

messages.author: .jrkirby
messages.content:
Prismata Incremental:

You play games against pacifist bot. You go first, you both have 6 drones and 2 engineers. Base set. Seems boring, right? But the game does not end when you destroy master bot's units. No, the game ends turn 20, no matter what. The goal? To get as much gold as possible in the bank before then.

But that's not the end. That, is the beginning. You enter a shop. All kinds of things to buy: new blueprints, extra units to start with, stuff like that. You can also increase the difficulty: both increasing the AI aggressiveness and skill, and also giving it a more powerful start position. Why? Doing so increases your multiplier exponentially increasing how much gold you get to take from the end of your net match into the shop. And of course the price of items in the shop has an exponential nature to it's pricing as well; to afford the expensive items you must either beat the bot on very difficult settings, and have a ton of gold left over, or you have to beat the bot over and over and over again.

This continues ad infinitum, as incremental games do, with progress getting slower and slower each time you complete the loop. When you are finally tired of it, you wonder why you spent 45 hours on something so pointless, there having been no real finale, no true goal, just an ever increasingly time consuming list of milestones you reached. There's another milestone you could reach, but it's been mathematically placed 3-4 more hours in the future and you've worn yourself out.
